directions.name: Restore Accepted PTX Branch And Tighten Export Path
directions.hypothesis: Round 41 is a clear negative result for explicit producer partitioning on the active 128x128 K16 PTX branch: runtime regressed from the accepted round-38 branch at 25.974272 ms (commit e26d834, run 20260420_012953_bf16_gemm_v1_e26d834) to 27.261951 ms, while mio stayed very low at 0.48% and barrier inflated to 14.61%. That makes Async Copy and Pg2s producer partitioning a reject for this round, not a reason to abandon the accepted PTX branch. The best next move is to restore that accepted full-B-reuse branch first and keep pushing the still-live Bank Conflict family on the export side: refine the padded c_shared scratch, pair packing, or warp-local export sequencing so the branch keeps its good feed behavior but spends fewer cycles in export-side shared traffic and sync.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory export overhead and warp-local synchronization in the PTX c_shared round-trip, now showing up mainly as barrier and short-scoreboard stalls rather than mio or DRAM starvation.
directions.risk: Medium. The accepted branch is already close to the current best, so export edits must preserve correctness, shared-memory budget, and the existing full-B-reuse feed path. Any extra sync or larger scratch can erase the gain immediately.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the restored accepted branch plus export refinement does not beat the restored baseline, or if short_scoreboard and barrier fail to move down together without increasing shared-memory footprint or register pressure.

directions.name: Restore Accepted PTX Branch And Rewrite Steady-State Sequencing
directions.hypothesis: The latest regression rejects one specific sequencing variant: splitting Pg2s producers into fixed A-only and B-only warps. It does not reject the broader Stage and Async Copy family. Because tensor-active stayed respectable at 47.46% and mio remained only 0.48%, the remaining upside is in the control plane around cp.async commit/wait and __syncthreads, not in deeper staging or tiling changes. Restore the accepted PTX branch first, then make the steady-state sequence more explicit while keeping the original all-warps staging helpers and the current two-stage depth, so the hot loop reduces unnecessary CTA coordination without reintroducing the round-41 producer partitioning penalty.
directions.expected_bottleneck: CTA-level synchronization and cp.async orchestration overhead in the active PTX steady-state loop, expressed as barrier inflation even when memory-issue pressure is already low.
directions.risk: Medium-high. Small wait-group or barrier-order mistakes can silently reduce overlap or break correctness, and this family already produced one strong regression when it over-constrained warp roles.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if a non-partitioned sequencing cleanup cannot recover to within noise of the accepted 25.974272 ms branch, or if barrier remains double-digit while mio stays low, because that means the chosen schedule is still over-coordinating the CTA.

directions.name: Restore Accepted PTX Branch And Apply A Narrow Consumer-Side Tweak
directions.hypothesis: Data Reuse, Register Reuse, and Ps2r still have signal on the accepted PTX path because full consumer-side B reuse was part of the best 25.974272 ms branch. The next compute-side attempt should therefore preserve that accepted full-B-reuse structure and only narrow the warp-local consume pattern, for example by trimming fragment residency or the mirrored-column sweep without falling back to the baseline helper and without any CTA repack. This keeps the low-mio benefit while trying to cut the remaining short-scoreboard and bank behavior inside the consumer path.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local fragment residency, consumer-side bank behavior, and short-scoreboard pressure inside the PTX 64x64 accumulate path.
directions.risk: High. This family has mixed evidence already, and even small consume-order changes can widen the live set, reintroduce barrier spillover, or destroy the accepted branch's current full-B-reuse win.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the narrower consumer-side tweak loses the accepted branch's low-mio behavior, or if short_scoreboard does not improve without sacrificing barrier and tensor-active together.

notes: Ranking is explicitly centered on restoring the accepted round-38 PTX branch first: commit e26d834 at 25.974272 ms remains the best known active-path base, and round 41's producer-partition Pg2s/Async Copy variant is a clean reject because it added about 1.287679 ms while pushing barrier to 14.61% even though mio stayed at 0.48%. Human-idea audit for this round: Tiling is rejected as the primary family because both the real active 256x128/64x64 path and the 128x128x32 path already regressed far beyond the accepted 128x128 K16 PTX base; Coalescing Access is deferred because global->shared already uses 16-byte cp.async and the latest run is not DRAM or mio limited; Data Reuse is accepted only in the already-proven full consumer-side B-reuse form on the accepted PTX branch; Async Copy is accepted only after restoring that branch, while explicit producer partitioning is rejected for this round; Bank Conflict is accepted as the primary family because export-path shared traffic and short-scoreboard remain live; L2 Cache is deferred because grouped-order experiments were already explored and the current regression is not L2-led; Register Reuse is accepted only as a bounded warp-local tweak that preserves full B reuse; Pg2s is rejected for producer partitioning and deferred for non-partitioned sequencing cleanup; Ps2r is deferred to the bounded consumer-side direction; Stage is accepted only as a narrow steady-state sequencing cleanup at the current depth, while deeper staging or extra CTA coordination is rejected. The goal remains <20 ms, not merely matching the 25.917889 ms CUTLASS baseline, so the ranking favors the highest-ceiling families still consistent with the accepted PTX branch rather than another tiling reset.
